title: DAY 1: Understanding Your Gear & Camera Fundamentals
objective: By the end of Day 1, you will know your camera’s basic settings (exposure, focus, frame rates) and be able to shoot a correctly exposed clip.

detailedContent.title: Your Camera – A Creative Tool, Not a Mystery
detailedContent.content:
**Any camera can make great videos.** The magic is not in the gear—it’s in how you use it. Today you’ll learn the essential settings that every videographer controls.

**The Exposure Triangle (Video Version)**

In photography, exposure is about still images. In video, it’s about motion, but the same three elements work together:

1. **Aperture (f‑stop)** – Controls how much light enters through the lens. It also affects **depth of field** (how blurry the background is).
   - Low f‑number (e.g., f/1.8) = more light, very blurry background (shallow depth of field)
   - High f‑number (e.g., f/16) = less light, everything in focus

2. **Shutter Speed** – In video, shutter speed is tied to frame rate. The **180‑degree rule** is your friend: set shutter speed to double your frame rate.
   - If shooting at 24 fps, shutter speed = 1/50
   - If shooting at 30 fps, shutter speed = 1/60
   - If shooting at 60 fps, shutter speed = 1/120

   This gives you natural motion blur. Too fast = choppy video; too slow = too much blur.

3. **ISO** – Your camera’s sensitivity to light.
   - Lower ISO (100–400) = cleaner image, needs more light
   - Higher ISO (800+) = brighter but introduces “noise” (grain)

detailedContent.title: Frame Rates & Why They Matter
detailedContent.content:
- **24 fps** – The “cinematic” standard. Used in movies.
- **30 fps** – Standard for TV and most online content.
- **60 fps** – Great for slow motion (when played back at 24 or 30 fps).

**Focus Modes**

- **Auto‑focus (AF):** Convenient, but can hunt or shift to wrong subject.
- **Manual focus (MF):** Gives you full control. Essential for interviews or when you want a rack focus.

**Resolution**

- **1080p (Full HD)** – Perfect for most uses, easier on storage.
- **4K** – Higher quality, allows cropping in post, but larger files.

**Pro Tip:** If you’re using a smartphone, download a manual camera app (like Filmic Pro or the native “Pro” mode) to control these settings.

video.title: Camera Basics for Video: Aperture, ISO & Shutter Speed Explained
video.description: Watch and take notes on how aperture, shutter speed, and ISO interact.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Get to Know Your Camera (15 minutes)
steps: Hold your camera/phone and locate the mode dial (if any)
Identify where to change aperture, ISO, and shutter speed
Find focus settings (AF/MF switch or on‑screen controls)
Write down the make/model of your camera and note any limitations

exercises.title: Exercise 2: The Exposure Triangle in Practice (20 minutes)
steps: Set up a simple scene with a subject (plant, toy, friend)
Set your camera to manual mode or use a manual video app
Choose a frame rate (e.g., 24 fps) and set shutter speed to double (1/50)
Adjust aperture and ISO for 0 exposure and record 10 seconds
Change settings individually to observe effects on brightness, blur, and noise

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Practice Focusing (10 minutes)
steps: Set up two objects at different distances from the camera
Use auto‑focus to switch between them and notice behavior
Switch to manual focus and practice pulling focus smoothly

reflectionPrompt: Which setting feels most confusing right now? What did I learn about my camera’s capabilities?

title: DAY 2: Composition & Framing
objective: By the end of Day 2, you will be able to compose visually compelling shots using essential framing rules.

detailedContent.title: The Art of Visual Storytelling
detailedContent.content:
Composition is how you arrange elements within the frame. Good composition guides the viewer’s eye and evokes emotion.

**The Rule of Thirds**

Imagine dividing your frame into a 3×3 grid. Place your subject along these lines or at their intersections.

- **Horizon lines:** Put the horizon on the top third or bottom third.
- **Eyes:** In a portrait, position the eyes on the top third line.

**Leading Lines**

Use natural lines in your scene (roads, fences, shadows) to lead the viewer’s eye toward your subject. Lines create depth and draw attention.

detailedContent.title: Headroom, Noseroom & Depth
detailedContent.content:
- **Headroom:** Space above the subject’s head. Too much looks small; too little feels cramped.
- **Noseroom:** Leave space in the direction the subject is facing.

**Depth**

Create depth by having:
- **Foreground** element (close to the lens)
- **Middle ground** (your subject)
- **Background** (what’s behind)

**Angles & Perspective**

- **Eye level:** Neutral, relatable.
- **Low angle:** Makes subject look powerful.
- **High angle:** Makes subject look small/vulnerable.

video.title: 8 Composition Techniques to Make Your Videos Look Professional
video.description: Demonstrates rule of thirds, leading lines, and depth techniques.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Grid Practice (15 minutes)
steps: Turn on the grid overlay in your camera/viewfinder
Frame at least 10 shots following the rule of thirds
Shoot a 5‑second clip for each

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Depth Exploration (10 minutes)
steps: Find a scene with clear foreground, middle, and background
Shoot without foreground, with subtle foreground, and bold foreground
Compare clips to see how depth changes the feel

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Angle Challenge (10 minutes)
steps: Pick one subject and shoot 5-second clips from eye level, low angle, and high angle
Observe the emotional difference in each clip

reflectionPrompt: Which composition technique feels most natural to me? Which one will I need to practice more?

title: DAY 3: Lighting Basics
objective: By the end of Day 3, you will understand how to shape light for video using natural and simple artificial sources.

detailedContent.title: Painting with Light
detailedContent.content:
Light is the raw material of videography. Great lighting can make a smartphone video look cinematic.

**Characteristics of Light**

1. **Intensity** – Brightness.
2. **Direction** – Front, side, back, top, bottom.
3. **Quality** – Hard (direct) or Soft (diffused).
4. **Color** – Measured in Kelvin (K). Daylight (~5600K) vs Tungsten (~3200K).

**Three‑Point Lighting (The Classic Setup)**

- **Key Light:** Main light, 45° angle, slightly above eye level.
- **Fill Light:** Fills shadows, less intense than key.
- **Back Light:** Separates subject from background, adds depth.

detailedContent.title: Natural Light Strategies
detailedContent.content:
**Golden Hour:** Hour after sunrise and before sunset. Soft and warm.

**Window Light:** Huge, soft light source. Position subject near window (key) and use white board for fill.

**Shooting Outdoors:** Avoid harsh midday sun. Find open shade or use a diffuser/reflector to manage harsh shadows.

video.title: Lighting for Video: Three‑Point Lighting Explained
video.description: Shows how to set up key, fill, and back lights using affordable gear.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Find and Observe Light (10 minutes)
steps: Observe how light changes by a window at different times
Notice light under a lamp with and without a shade
Check rooms with multiple light sources

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Three‑Point Lighting Setup (20 minutes)
steps: Set up key light at 45° to subject
Add fill light to soften shadows
Add back light behind subject pointing at shoulders
Compare clips with each light added one by one

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Natural Light Portrait (10 minutes)
steps: Position subject near a window as the only light source
Add a reflector on the shadow side and shoot again
Compare the difference in shadow quality

reflectionPrompt: What was the biggest lighting challenge I faced today? How did adding a fill light change the image?

title: DAY 4: Audio for Video
objective: By the end of Day 4, you will understand why audio matters more than video and be able to capture clean sound using basic techniques.

detailedContent.title: Your Audience Listens First
detailedContent.content:
Bad audio ruins good video. Viewers will tolerate mediocre visuals but will click away immediately from poor sound.

**Solutions for Better Audio**

1. **External microphone:** Lavalier (lapel) for interviews, Shotgun for directionality.
2. **Audio recorder:** A smartphone placed close to the subject works too.
3. **Indoor acoustics:** Soft furnishings (rugs, curtains) absorb sound and reduce echo.

**Mic Technique**

- **Distance:** Mic should be no more than 18 inches from subject.
- **Levels:** Aim for peaks around -12dB to -6dB.
- **Monitor:** Always use headphones to catch buzzing or wind noise.

video.title: The Ultimate Guide to Audio for Video
video.description: Covers mic types, placement, and recording levels.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Listen to Your Environment (10 minutes)
steps: Sit quietly and identify ambient noises (HVAC, traffic)
Record 30 seconds of room ambient sound
Listen back on headphones to establish a baseline

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Distance Test (10 minutes)
steps: Record speaking from 1 foot, 3 feet, and 6 feet away
Listen how room echo takes over as distance increases

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Improve a Room’s Sound (10 minutes)
steps: Record in a bare room, then add blankets/pillows and record again
Notice the reduction in echo and improved clarity

reflectionPrompt: What surprised me most about today’s audio experiments? What’s the biggest audio challenge in my usual shooting environment?

title: DAY 5: Camera Movement & Shooting Techniques
objective: By the end of Day 5, you will be able to execute basic camera moves with intention and understand when to use each.

detailedContent.title: Motivated Movement
detailedContent.content:
Movement should have a reason—to reveal, follow action, or create energy. 

**Basic Camera Moves**

1. **Pan:** Horizontal swivel following a subject.
2. **Tilt:** Vertical swivel revealing height.
3. **Dolly / Track:** Physical movement toward/away or sideways.
4. **Handheld:** High energy, immersive (use bent knees for stability).

**The 5‑Second Rule**
Hold each shot for at least 5 seconds for editing flexibility.

**Shoot for the Edit**
Capture Wide shots (location), Medium shots (action), Close‑ups (detail), and Cutaways.

video.title: Camera Movement Techniques: When and How to Move
video.description: Demonstrates each basic camera move with cinematic examples.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Tripod Practice (10 minutes)
steps: Practice smooth pans with picked start and end points
Practice smooth vertical tilts
Review footage for jerks and repeat until smooth

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Handheld Walk (10 minutes)
steps: Walk while holding camera, keeping horizon level
Practice the 'ninja walk' with bent knees as shock absorbers
Record a clip walking toward a subject and review stability

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Shoot a Simple Sequence (10 minutes)
steps: Pick an activity (like making tea) and shoot a sequence:
Wide shot of environment, Medium of the action
Close-up of detail, Cutaway of a related object

reflectionPrompt: Which camera movement felt most natural? How can I add purposeful movement to my Day 7 video?

title: DAY 6: Introduction to Editing
objective: By the end of Day 6, you will be able to import footage, perform basic cuts, add music, and export a video using free editing software.

detailedContent.title: Editing Workflow
detailedContent.content:
Editing is the final rewrite. 

1. **Import & Organize:** Rename clips descriptively.
2. **Rough Cut:** Arrange clips on timeline and trim.
3. **Add Audio:** Balance levels and add royalty-free music.
4. **Color & Effects:** Simple exposure/white balance correction.
5. **Export:** 1080p, H.264 is the standard.

**Editing Principles**

- **Pacing:** Match cut speed to mood.
- **J‑cut / L‑cut:** Audio starts/ends at different time than video for smoothness.
- **Transitions:** Simple cuts are usually best.

video.title: How to Edit Videos for Beginners (Step‑by‑Step)
video.description: Covers basic workflow from import to export.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Install & Explore Software (15 minutes)
steps: Choose/install software (DaVinci Resolve, CapCut, iMovie)
Import clips and explore the interface (timeline, tools)

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Make a Rough Cut (15 minutes)
steps: Use the Day 5 sequence and arrange clips on timeline
Trim clips and ensure they tell a mini-story

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Add Music & Export (10 minutes)
steps: Download/add royalty-free music and balance levels
Export at 1080p H.264 and watch your mini-video

reflectionPrompt: How did music change the feel of the video? What’s one editing skill I want to learn next?

title: DAY 7: Putting It All Together
objective: By the end of Day 7, you will have planned, shot, and edited a complete short video (30–60 seconds) demonstrating all the skills you’ve learned.

detailedContent.title: Planning & Production
detailedContent.content:
**Pre‑Production:** Choose a simple concept (mini-vlog, tutorial). Write a shot list with Wide, Medium, and Close-up shots.

**Production:** Follow your shot list, holding each for 5+ seconds. Capture extra cutaways and 30 seconds of room tone.

**Post‑Production:** Import, Rough Cut, Fine Cut, Audio mixing, Color correction, and Export.

video.title: How to Plan, Shoot, and Edit a Video in One Day
video.description: An inspirational look at an entire video project from start to finish.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Plan Your Video (15 minutes)
steps: Choose concept and write a 8-12 shot list
Gather any needed props

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Shoot Your Video (30 minutes)
steps: Set up shots according to list, capture detailed cutaways
Record silent room tone

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Edit & Evaluate (30 minutes)
steps: Edit full sequence with music and audio cleanup
Evaluate strengths and growth areas compared to Day 1

reflectionPrompt: What was the most important thing I learned this week? What is my next step?
